text: combined laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy for gastric gist and sling-preserving distal pancreatectomy for mucinous cystic lesion of the pancreas in an elderly patient. The authors have nothing to disclose. Our patient is an eighty-seven-year-old male who presented with anemia. His upper GI endoscopy identified large submucosal mass in the fundus close to the GE junction. Biopsy confirmed gastrointestinal stromal tumor. Of note, his current PMI is twenty-six point six. As you can see, the tumor in relation to the GE junction in this retroflexion view of the upper GI endoscopy. A CT scan confirmed the presence of this large mass in the fundus close to the GE junction as well as a two point five centimeter complex pancreatic cyst in the tail of the pancreas with some high risk features. Further evaluation by PET scan identified no evidence of metastasis.

Upon entry to the abdominal cavity, the surgeon identifies a large gastrointestinal stromal tumor in the fundus with no evidence of liver or peritoneal metastasis. A liver retractor is placed, and upper GI endoscopy is performed to ensure negative margin resection. The greater curvature of the stomach is mobilized using a ligature device, adhering to oncological principles. The angle of His is carefully mobilized until the left diaphragmatic crus is identified. Under endoscopic guidance, sleeve gastrectomy is initiated, and partial gastric resection is performed using multiple firings of an endo-GIA stapler. The transected gastric tissue is buttressed with V-lock sutures, and repeat endoscopy confirms a wide sleeve gastrectomy with no twist or narrowing.

The surgeon mobilizes the lower edge of the pancreas from the transverse mesocolon and dissects it from the retroperitoneum using a Ligasure device. The upper edge of the pancreas is then exposed, and the splenic artery and vein are identified and dissected. Multiple small blood vessels from the splenic artery and tributaries from the splenic vein are controlled using the Ligasure device. After complete mobilization of the distal tail of the pancreas, an endo-GIA stapler is used to transect the tail. Specimens are placed in an endo bag, and a GVAC drain is placed in the surgical field after securing hemostasis.

In this video, we showed that combined laparoscopic procedures can be safely done even in elderly patients if surgical expertise available. This is to ensure patients will have the benefits of laparoscopy, i.e. faster recovery with lower surgical trauma. Partial sleep gastrectomy is a valid option for resection of proximal gastric gist close to the GE junction with good postoperative functional outcomes.
